section.title: They Have Afflicted Me from My Youth
section.sectionRationale: A Song of Ascents, tenth in the pilgrim collection Psalms 120 to 134, placed in Book V of the Psalter, Psalms 107 to 150. Israel looks back over her long history of affliction, from Egypt onward, and confesses that though deeply wounded, she has never been destroyed, because the LORD Himself has cut her free.
section.thesis: Israel's long history of affliction has left real scars, yet the LORD's righteousness has kept His people from being overcome and will bring lasting shame on those who hate Zion, a pattern of suffering followed by vindication fulfilled in Christ's own affliction and triumph.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the long history of affliction survived, verses 1 to 3, the LORD's righteous deliverance, verse 4, and the closing prayer that the enemies of Zion would wither in shame, verses 5 to 8.

units.label: They have not prevailed against me

verses.text: Many a time they have persecuted me from my youth, let Israel now declare,
many a time they have persecuted me from my youth, but they have not prevailed against me.
The plowmen plowed over my back; they made their furrows long.

units.label: He has cut me from the cords of the wicked

verses.text: The LORD is righteous; He has cut me from the {{avoth|cords}} of the wicked.

units.label: May all who hate Zion be turned back in shame

verses.text: May all who hate Zion be turned back in shame.
May they be like grass on the rooftops, which withers before it can grow,
unable to fill the hands of the reaper, or the arms of the binder of sheaves.
May none who pass by say to them, "The blessing of the LORD be on you; we bless you in the name of the LORD."

avoth.gloss: Cords, ropes, or thick bindings, used elsewhere for a yoke's harness; the LORD's righteousness is shown precisely in cutting the cords that bound His people to the wicked, verse 4.

basic.context: This Song of Ascents rehearses Israel's long history of affliction, from the persecution of her youth in Egypt onward, and confesses that despite deep, real wounds, she has survived because the LORD is righteous and has cut her free, then prays that those who hate Zion would wither like shallow rooftop grass.
basic.hermeneutics: It describes Israel's national history of survived affliction and it prescribes as an abiding pattern honest acknowledgment of real suffering alongside confident trust in the LORD's righteous deliverance and prayer for the ultimate defeat of His people's enemies.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalm turns on avoth, cords or bindings, cut by the LORD in verse 4, the vivid image of a yoke or rope of bondage snapped by His righteous intervention, freeing His afflicted people.
basic.typology: The plowed back of Israel anticipates the scourging of Christ, and the declaration 'they have not prevailed' anticipates His resurrection victory over every power that sought to destroy Him.
basic.christ: Jesus was scourged before His crucifixion, John 19:1, His back plowed as Israel's was, yet death did not prevail against Him, and at the cross He decisively cut the cords of sin, death, and the devil that bound His people, disarming every hostile power, Colossians 2:15.
basic.god: The LORD is righteous, and His righteousness is displayed precisely in cutting the cords of the wicked that bind His afflicted people, delivering them not because they deserved it but because He is faithful to His covenant.
basic.crossReferences: John 19:1 shows Jesus scourged, His back plowed as Israel's was, prior to His victorious resurrection. Colossians 2:15 shows Christ disarming rulers and authorities, cutting every binding cord of sin and death. Romans 8:37 declares believers more than conquerors through Him who loved them.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's pattern of long affliction followed by the LORD's righteous deliverance is fulfilled in Christ, scourged and afflicted yet never overcome, who cuts the cords of sin and death for His people and secures their final victory over every enemy.

context.literary: A Song of Ascents structured around a communal remembrance, verses 1 to 3, a confessional turning point, verse 4, and an imprecatory prayer against Zion's enemies, verses 5 to 8, using vivid agricultural imagery throughout.
context.historical: The repeated phrase 'from my youth' points back to Israel's earliest history, likely the oppression in Egypt, and the psalm's language of persecution fits the many later waves of hostility from Philistines, Assyrians, and Babylonians as well.
context.canonical: Follows Psalm 128's picture of ordinary household blessing with an honest reckoning of Israel's national suffering, and anticipates the New Testament's pattern of affliction endured yet not overcome in Christ and His people.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To lead God's people in honest acknowledgment of long, real suffering while confessing that the LORD's righteousness has always kept them from destruction, and to pray that those who hate Zion would be shamed and rendered fruitless.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes historic Israel's repeated survival of affliction. It prescribes as an abiding pattern honest lament over real suffering paired with confident trust in God's righteous deliverance, and prayer, not personal vengeance, against those who set themselves against God's people.

debates.question: How should Christians read the imprecatory prayer of verses 5 to 8, that enemies of Zion wither in shame?

views.label: A prayer for God's public vindication, not personal revenge
views.case: The prayer asks God to act justly and publicly against persistent enemies of His people and purposes, a legitimate longing for justice rather than personal vengeance, which Scripture elsewhere forbids.

views.label: A prayer requiring careful, gospel-shaped adaptation
views.case: Since Christ commands love and prayer for enemies, believers should pray this psalm's substance, longing for evil to be overturned, while also praying for the repentance and salvation of those currently opposed to God's people.

debates.bearing: Either way, believers may pray for God's justice against persistent, unrepentant evil while also, in Christ's name, hoping and praying for the enemy's repentance rather than harboring personal hatred.

study.originalLanguages: The psalm's turning point is avoth, cords or bindings, cut decisively by the righteous LORD in verse 4, an image of a yoke or rope of oppression snapped, freeing His long-afflicted people from bondage to the wicked.

typology.type: The plowed back of persecuted Israel, verse 3
typology.fulfillment: Christ scourged before His crucifixion, His back likewise plowed, John 19:1.

typology.type: The LORD cutting the cords of the wicked, verse 4
typology.fulfillment: Christ disarming rulers and authorities at the cross, cutting every cord of sin and death, Colossians 2:15.

study.christ: Jesus was scourged before His crucifixion, His back plowed as Israel's was plowed by her persecutors, John 19:1, yet like Israel's confession that 'they have not prevailed against me,' death itself could not prevail against Him. At the cross He decisively cut the cords that bound His people, sin, death, and the accusing power of the devil, disarming rulers and authorities and triumphing over them, Colossians 2:15, so that believers are, in Him, more than conquerors, Romans 8:37.
study.god: The LORD is righteous, and His righteousness is displayed precisely in cutting the cords of the wicked that bind His afflicted people, delivering them not because of their own merit but because of His covenant faithfulness across every generation of suffering.

crossReferences.note: Jesus scourged before His crucifixion, His back plowed as Israel's was, yet raised to life, unprevailed against.
Christ disarms rulers and authorities at the cross, cutting every cord of sin and death that bound His people.
Believers are more than conquerors through Christ, the fulfillment of this psalm's confidence that affliction does not have the last word.

study.oneStory: This psalm's pattern of long affliction followed by the LORD's righteous deliverance is fulfilled in Christ, scourged and afflicted yet never ultimately overcome, who cuts the cords of sin and death for His people and secures their final victory over every enemy of Zion.

sermon.title: That's Life!
sermon.why: Begg treats Psalms 129 to 131 together, showing how this psalm's honest reckoning with long affliction leads into the trust and humility of the psalms that follow.

quiz.q: What does Israel declare in verses 1 to 2 about her long history of persecution?

options.text: Many times she has been persecuted from her youth, yet enemies have not prevailed against her.
options.note: This is the exact double declaration of the opening verses.

options.text: She has never once faced any persecution.
options.note: The psalm explicitly describes repeated, long-standing persecution, not its absence.

options.text: Her enemies eventually destroyed her completely.
options.note: The psalm insists the opposite, that enemies have not prevailed.

options.text: She has only faced persecution in recent memory.
options.note: The phrase 'from my youth' points to a very long, ongoing history, not merely recent events.

quiz.q: What image does verse 3 use to describe the suffering Israel endured?

options.text: Plowmen plowing over her back, making long furrows.
options.note: This vivid, painful image is the exact picture the psalm uses for deep, real suffering.

options.text: A gentle breeze passing over a field.
options.note: The image is one of deep, painful wounding, not something gentle or mild.

options.text: A brief shadow that quickly passed.
options.note: The furrows are described as long, suggesting sustained and severe suffering, not a brief shadow.

options.text: A minor scratch quickly healed.
options.note: The imagery of plowed furrows suggests deep, lasting wounds, not a minor injury.

quiz.q: What does verse 4 say the LORD has done?

options.text: Being righteous, He has cut Israel free from the cords of the wicked.
options.note: This is the psalm's turning point, crediting Israel's deliverance to the LORD's righteous act.

options.text: He has ignored Israel's suffering entirely.
options.note: The verse credits the LORD directly with decisive, righteous deliverance.

options.text: He has increased the cords binding Israel to her enemies.
options.note: The verse describes the LORD cutting, not increasing, the cords of bondage.

options.text: He has delayed any response for centuries.
options.note: The verse presents the LORD's righteous action as decisive, not as an indefinite delay.

quiz.q: What does the psalm pray will happen to those who hate Zion, according to verses 5 to 7?

options.text: That they be turned back in shame, like rooftop grass that withers before it can grow.
options.note: This is the exact imprecatory prayer and imagery of the closing verses.

options.text: That they would prosper and multiply endlessly.
options.note: The prayer is for their shame and fruitlessness, the opposite of prosperity.

options.text: That they would be given a permanent alliance with Israel.
options.note: The prayer is for their defeat and shame, not for alliance or partnership.

options.text: That they would receive a slow, gradual decline over centuries.
options.note: The image of rooftop grass withering 'before it can grow' suggests a swift, not slow, futility.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's pattern of affliction followed by deliverance find fulfillment in Christ?

options.text: Jesus was scourged like Israel's plowed back, yet death did not prevail against Him, and He disarmed every hostile power at the cross.
options.note: John 19:1 and Colossians 2:15 show this exact pattern fulfilled decisively in Christ.

options.text: Christ avoided all suffering entirely.
options.note: The New Testament clearly describes Christ's real suffering, including scourging before crucifixion.

options.text: Christ's suffering ended in permanent defeat with no vindication.
options.note: The resurrection is the decisive vindication that answers His suffering, the opposite of permanent defeat.

options.text: The pattern applies only to ancient Israel and has no connection to Christ.
options.note: The New Testament draws a direct line from this pattern of affliction and deliverance to Christ's own suffering and victory.

questions: The psalm honestly names deep, long-standing suffering, plowed furrows on the back, without denying its reality. Where do you need to bring honest lament, rather than forced positivity, before God this week?
Despite real affliction, Israel confesses 'they have not prevailed against me.' What battle in your life feels ongoing, and how does this psalm's confidence in ultimate survival encourage you?
The LORD's righteousness is shown in cutting the cords of the wicked. What binding sin, fear, or circumstance do you need Christ to cut you free from right now?

The psalm prays for the shame and fruitlessness of those who hate Zion, echoing Christ's call to also pray for enemies' repentance. How can you pray for God's justice while also longing for your opponents' salvation?

Christ's own scourged back and ultimate resurrection victory fulfill this psalm's pattern. How does knowing that Jesus endured worse affliction than you, and triumphed, strengthen your endurance today?

translationNotes.note: Many a time can also be rendered often or repeatedly, emphasizing the recurring, not singular, nature of Israel's persecution across her history.
The image of plowmen plowing over the back reflects ancient Near Eastern scourging practices, turning the language of agriculture into a picture of severe physical suffering.
Grass on the rooftops grew in the thin layer of soil on flat Middle Eastern roofs, sprouting quickly after rain but withering just as fast in the sun, an image of shallow, short-lived futility.

meta.notes: BSB text taken verbatim from biblehub.com's BSB page for Psalm 129 (the local makor-scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error, so the web fallback was used for all of Psalms 124-132). Verse count (8) and wording cross-checked against the section map bounds Psalms 129:1-8. Two em dashes in verse 1 were converted to commas; no other em or en dashes present. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming Alistair Begg's sermon 'That's Life!' (covering Psalms 129-131) at truthforlife.org.
